standard_description: Understand that shapes in different categories (e.g., rhombuses, rectangles, and others) may share attributes (e.g., having four sides), and that the shared attributes can define a larger category (e.g., quadrilaterals). Recognize rhombuses, rectangles, and squares as examples of quadrilaterals, and draw examples of quadrilaterals that do not belong to any of these subcategories.
sources: Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
Van Hiele, P.M. (1986). Structure and Insight: A Theory of Mathematics Education. Academic Press.
Battista, M.T. (2007). The development of geometric and spatial thinking. NCTM Handbook.
NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Geometry K–6, 2012
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 7 Topic E — Polygon Classification, Teacher Edition, 2013
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.description: Student identifies a shape only when it is in the textbook prototype orientation (e.g., square sitting flat, triangle pointing up). Rotated or unusual orientations are rejected as 'not that shape.'
misconceptions.example: A square rotated 45° (looks like a diamond). Student says it is a 'rhombus' or 'diamond,' not a square.
misconceptions.citation: Van Hiele (1986) p.62 — 'At Level 1 (Visual), children recognize shapes by overall appearance, not properties; a rotated square is no longer "a square" because it doesn\'t look like the prototype. Progression to Level 2 (Analysis) requires attending to properties (4 equal sides, 4 right angles).'
misconceptions.remediation: Define shapes by PROPERTIES, not appearance: square = 4 equal sides + 4 right angles. Rotate paper squares — same shape regardless of orientation. Practice identifying shapes in various orientations.

misconceptions.short_label: Treating shape categories as mutually exclusive
misconceptions.description: Student thinks a square is not a rectangle (or a rectangle is not a parallelogram). Doesn\'t understand inclusive hierarchy: square ⊂ rectangle ⊂ parallelogram ⊂ quadrilateral.
misconceptions.example: 'Is a square also a rectangle?' Student answers no. Reasoning: 'It\'s a square, not a rectangle.'
misconceptions.citation: Battista (2007) p.866 — 'Children initially treat shape categories as mutually exclusive partitions; understanding the inclusive hierarchy (square IS a rectangle IS a parallelogram) requires Van Hiele Level 3 abstraction.'
misconceptions.remediation: Inclusive logic: A square has all rectangle properties (4 sides, 4 right angles, opposite sides parallel and equal) PLUS extra (all 4 sides equal). So a square IS a rectangle (and more). Use Venn diagrams.

misconceptions.short_label: Miscounting sides or vertices of a polygon
misconceptions.description: Student miscounts the sides (or equivalently, vertices) of a polygon. Common on shapes with 5+ sides or irregular shapes.
misconceptions.example: Hexagon (6 sides). Student counts 5 sides, misses one. Result: classifies as pentagon.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Geometry Progressions K-6 (2012) p.10 — 'Children miscount sides and vertices, especially on shapes with 5+ sides; teaching to mark each side or vertex with a tick or number before counting prevents errors.'
misconceptions.remediation: Mark each vertex 1, 2, 3, … going around the shape. Total marks = number of sides. Practice on triangles up through octagons.

misconceptions.description: Student attributes properties of one shape to a related shape incorrectly. E.g., assumes all parallelograms have right angles because rectangles do.
misconceptions.example: 'All parallelograms have 4 right angles.' Student says true (only rectangles do).
misconceptions.citation: Van Hiele (1986) p.108 — 'At Level 2 (Analysis), children identify properties but may overgeneralize, transferring properties of a familiar shape (rectangle) to a less familiar related shape (parallelogram).'
misconceptions.remediation: List properties for each shape category separately. Compare: parallelogram has opposite sides parallel and equal, but NOT necessarily right angles. Use property tables.

misconceptions.description: Student calls open figures (with gaps) polygons. Doesn\'t recognize that polygons must be CLOSED (start and end at the same point).
misconceptions.example: A shape with 4 line segments that don\'t meet to form a closed figure. Student calls it a quadrilateral.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.456 — 'The closed-figure requirement of polygons is often overlooked; children call any sequence of connected line segments a polygon. Explicit teaching that polygons must close (form a complete boundary) is needed.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Polygon definition: CLOSED figure made of straight line segments. Trace boundary with finger — must end where it started. Open figures are NOT polygons.

misconceptions.description: Student includes shapes with curved sides as polygons. Doesn\'t understand that polygon sides must be STRAIGHT line segments.
misconceptions.example: Half-circle or shape with one curved side. Student calls it a polygon.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Geometry Progressions K-6 (2012) p.10 — 'Some children include curved-side shapes (semicircles, ovals) as polygons; the requirement that all sides be straight line segments must be explicit.'
misconceptions.remediation: Polygon sides: STRAIGHT only. Check each side: trace it — if any curve, not a polygon. Practice classifying mix of straight-sided and curved-sided shapes.

misconceptions.short_label: Believing larger shapes are 'different' from smaller versions
misconceptions.description: Student believes a large triangle is a different shape than a small triangle, or that size affects classification.
misconceptions.example: Two triangles, one large and one tiny. Student says they are 'different shapes' because of size.
misconceptions.citation: Van Hiele (1986) p.65 — 'Beginning learners use overall appearance including size to classify; size-invariance of shape names is a conceptual achievement.'
misconceptions.remediation: Shape name depends on SIDES and ANGLES, not size. A 1-inch triangle and a 100-foot triangle are both triangles. Practice with shapes of varying sizes.
